key_insight: The paper introduces second-order PFEMs for surface diffusion that exactly conserve enclosed area in 2D and volume in 3D by using Simpson--Boole geometric identities along a quadratic time path.
engineering_relevance: These schemes enable accurate long-time interface evolution with built-in geometric conservation, making them attractive for computational mechanics and moving-boundary simulations without extra constraint multipliers.
method: Build a quadratic temporal interpolation of the evolving curve/surface.
Use exact geometric variation identities to evaluate area change by Simpson's rule in 2D and volume change by Boole's rule in 3D.
Assemble the fully discrete method on BGN-predicted auxiliary geometries to remain compatible with second-order implementations.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments showing second-order convergence, exact area/volume conservation, and good mesh quality for both 2D curves and 3D surfaces.
watch_item: The method relies on accurate BGN-predicted auxiliary geometries and quadratic time interpolation, so implementation quality may affect robustness on challenging meshes or highly nonlinear evolutions.
paper_title: Second-Order Area/Volume-Preserving PFEMs for Surface Diffusion via Simpson--Boole Geometric Identities
